coredata.dc:title: Quantitative determination of Penicillamine in pharmaceutical samples by Copper-Modified Drug-Based carbon dots utilizing a turn ON-OFF-ON strategy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdulhameed, Baraa Ramzi
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, a sensitive and simple method is developed for sensing and quantifying Penicillamine (PA) in pharmaceutical samples utilizing a copper-conjugated carbon dots probe. The proposed method employed a turn ON-OFF-ON strategy with carbon dots fluorescence quenching by addition of copper and fluorescence recovery of up to 91 % by addition of PA. Expired moxifloxacin tablet powder was used as a novel carbon precursor during the synthesis of the carbon dots (MOX–CDs) which involved a single-step solvothermal procedure. The prepared MOX–CDs demonstrated excitation independent yellowish green fluorescence emission (Quantum yield ≈ 51 %) with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths being observed at 410 nm and 510 nm respectively. The suggested strategy for assaying PA offered a detection limit of 0.14 µM (S/N = 3) and a linear response from 2.5 µM to 50 µM (R2 = 0.9987). In addition to offering merits like accuracy and sensitivity, the proposed probe also provides a method for solid waste treatment by reprocessing expired moxifloxacin powder.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence recovery
Turn ON-OFF-ON
Pharmaceutical analysis
Carbon dots
Moxifloxacin
Penicillamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of standard and real samples of Penicillamine
Effect of Cu2+ on the MOX–CDs and fabrication of MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe
Sensing of Penicillamine with the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe
Selectivity of the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe for Penicillamine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of (MOX–CDs), quenching by Cu2+ (formation of MOX–CDs–Cu2+ complex), and detection of PA (formation of PA–Cu2+ complex and release of MOX–CDs).

(A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) photograph of the MOX–CDs [Inset: Particle size distribution histogram]. (B) Higher-magnification transmission electron microscopy (TEM) photograph of the MOX–CDs [Inset: High-resolution image of the marked carbon dot]. (C) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of the MOX–CDs [Inset: Particles height along the marked line]. (D) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the MOX–CDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the MOX–CDs (A) Survey spectra. (B) Deconvoluted C 1 s spectra. (C) Deconvoluted O 1 s spectra. (D) Deconvoluted N 1 s spectra. (E) Deconvoluted P 2p spectra.

(A) Fourier transformed infrared spectra of the MOX–CDs (Red) and EXP–MOX (Green). (B) Raman spectra of the MOX–CDs. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of MOX–CDs (Red) and EXP–MOX (Green) [Inset: digital image of the MOX–CDs aqueous solution in UV light (right) and visible light (left)]. (D) Fluorescence graph showing the excitation (Red) and emission (Blue) spectra of the MOX–CDs, as well as the excitation (Yellow) and emission (Green) spectra of the EXP–MOX. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of MOX–CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Stability studies of the MOX–CDs in different (A) pH, (B) Ionic strength, and (C) Temperature.

(A) Effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of MOX–CDs. (B) Effect of different pharmaceutical excipients and PA on the fluorescence intensity of MOX–CDs. (C) Quenching of the MOX–CDs by different concentrations of CuCl2. (D) Plot of fluorescence intensity recovery by addition of various concentrations of PA to the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe. (E) The plot of (F/F0) values of the MOX–CDs –Cu2+ probe with increasing concentrations of PA.
The effect of various pharmaceutical excipients and PA on the fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe.
Summary of various methods for the assay of Penicillamine.

head.ce:title: Quantitative determination of Penicillamine in pharmaceutical samples by Copper-Modified Drug-Based carbon dots utilizing a turn ON-OFF-ON strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Branch of Medical Education, College of Medicine, University of Sulaimani, Sulaymaniyah City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Branch of Medical Education, College of Medicine, University of Sulaimani, Sulaymaniyah City, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, a sensitive and simple method is developed for sensing and quantifying Penicillamine (PA) in pharmaceutical samples utilizing a copper-conjugated carbon dots probe. The proposed method employed a turn ON-OFF-ON strategy with carbon dots fluorescence quenching by addition of copper and fluorescence recovery of up to 91 % by addition of PA. Expired moxifloxacin tablet powder was used as a novel carbon precursor during the synthesis of the carbon dots (MOX–CDs) which involved a single-step solvothermal procedure. The prepared MOX–CDs demonstrated excitation independent yellowish green fluorescence emission (Quantum yield ≈ 51 %) with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths being observed at 410 nm and 510 nm respectively. The suggested strategy for assaying PA offered a detection limit of 0.14 µM (S/N = 3) and a linear response from 2.5 µM to 50 µM (R2 = 0.9987). In addition to offering merits like accuracy and sensitivity, the proposed probe also provides a method for solid waste treatment by reprocessing expired moxifloxacin powder.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Penicillamine (PA) is a chelating drug used mostly for the treatment of metal toxicity, especially copper toxicity (Wilson's disease) [1]. It is the resulting metabolite of penicillin antibiotics hydrolytic degradation and it exists in two enantiomeric forms: Levorotatory form (L-PA) and dextrorotatory form (D-PA), with the dextrorotatory (D-PA) form being the clinically active one as the Levorotatory form (L-PA) is extremely toxic [2–5]. It can also be used for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, and cystinuria while it is listed on the World Health Organization's list of essential medicines [6–9]. Nevertheless, PA can cause many adverse effects such as thrombocytopenia, proteinuria, dysgeusia rash, myasthenia gravis, nausea, and diarrhea [10–13].

Accordingly, close monitoring of PA in pharmaceutical samples is of vital importance and many methods have been reported in the literature for this purpose, which include: colorimetry, voltammetry, fluorometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, and flow injection analysis [14–19]. These methods offered different levels of success in terms of sensitivity and detection limit; however, they suffered from drawbacks such as expensive equipment, high running costs, long analysis time, and vulnerability to matrix effects limiting their benefits for routine-based analysis. As a result, it is imperative to develop new methods for the assay of PA that offer high selectivity, sensitivity, and low detection limit while maintaining simplicity, low running costs, and brief analysis time.

Over the previous years, highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively utilized in many fields such as optoelectronics, bio-chemical sensing, drug delivery, and pharmaceutical analysis [20–25]. The high success level achieved in applications involving CDs can be attributed to their outstanding properties such as high biocompatibility, excellent photostability, facile preparation, low costs, and ease of surface modification [26–30]. The synthesis of CDs can be achieved either with top-down or with bottom-up methods [31]. Generally, such methods involve using organic precursors such as citric acid, uric acid, or glucose as starting materials to produce CDs [32–34]. Using unconventional carbon precursors such as expired drugs for the synthesis of CDs has been scarcely reported in previous studies in which it was exploited for sensing metal ions [35]. The main advantage of this approach is to minimize pharmaceutical waste mitigating its environmental impact on natural water resources that is not only hazardous but can lead to complications like antibiotic resistance [36,37]. Another potential benefit includes cost efficiency as waste-derived CDs are cheap and readily available not to mention that this approach will minimize the need for expensive disposal processes [38]. Consequently, this pharmaceutical-waste recycling approach possesses promising potential and it should be studied further while emphasizing the applications of such value-added nanomaterials in unexplored fields such as pharmaceutical analysis.

Herein, we used expired moxifloxacin tablets powder as a carbon precursor for synthesizing highly fluorescent carbon dots (MOX–CDs) by a single-step solvothermal method. The produced MOX–CDs can be selectively quenched by copper ions forming a complex (MOX–CDs–Cu2+) and this complex can be further used for the detection and quantification of PA by recovery of fluorescence as a turn ON-OFF-ON probe with the formation of a new complex of (PA–Cu2+). The proposed approach contributes to environmental sustainability, as it reprocesses the pharmaceutical waste of moxifloxacin powder into carbon dots instead of disposing of it which has not been reported in the literature to the best of our knowledge. The scheme of MOX–CDs preparation, quenching by Cu2+, and fluorescence recovery by PA is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Pure Penicillamine (>98.0 %) was purchased from TCI Chemicals (Hong Kong, China), while phosphoric acid (99.0 %), sodium hydroxide (99.0 %), acetic acid (>99.0 %), sodium acetate (>99.0 %), all the biomolecules, and metal salts used were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Expired moxifloxacin tablets and real samples of PA capsules (Cupripen®) were obtained from a local pharmacy. All reagents used in this paper were of analytical grade and were used without further purification while deionized water was used for preparation of aqueous solutions throughout the producers.

The size and morphology of the MOX–CDs were studied using a TECNAI G2 F20 (USA) high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) by setting the acceleration voltage to 200 kV. Furthermore, the topography images of the MOX–CDs were obtained using a Nanosurf Flex-Axiom (Switzerland) atomic force microscope (AFM) under ambient conditions while using a FluidFM cantilever holder. The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were collected to study the crystallinity of the MOX–CDs with the utilization of an Empyrean X-ray Diffractometer (Netherlands) equipped with thin film attachment while setting the voltage at 45 kV. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using an Agilent Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (USA) equipped with 1 cm quartz cuvettes at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrum was obtained using an Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (USA) with a xenon arc lamp while setting the excitations and emission slits at 5.0 nm. To determine the functional groups on the surface of the MOX–CDs a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 (USA) Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer was used while equipping it with a diamond ATR attachment. A Renishaw inVia Reflex Raman Microscope (UK) was used to generate the Raman spectra while equipping it with Nd:YAG laser source at 785 nm. Furthermore, a Thermo Scientific Thermo Escalab 250 XI (USA) was utilized to generate the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data while using CasaXPS® software (version 2.3.25) to analyze the results. Measurements of pH were conducted using a Jenway 3510 bench-top pH meter (UK) equipped with glass electrodes.

The synthesis of MOX–CDs was achieved utilizing a single-step solvothermal method. In brief, 20 tablets of expired moxifloxacin were ground into a fine powder, and 0.1 g of that powder was mixed with 10 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid until a clear solution was formed. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and placed in a drying oven for 8 h at 180 °C. The autoclave was allowed to cool at ambient temperature then the resulting solution was filtered utilizing a 0.22 μm filter membrane to separate any large particles which was followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the MOX–CDs brown solution was dialyzed through a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane for 24 h against double-distilled water. The final product was stored at ambient temperature for further analytical purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard and real samples of Penicillamine
ce:section.ce:para: The standard PA stock solution was prepared by dissolving 14.92 mg of pure PA with 10 mL of deionized water then transferring it to a 1 L volumetric flask then adding deionized water up to the mark. The real pharmaceutical PA sample was prepared by taking the content of 20 capsules of Cupripen® and grinding it into a fine powder. A suitable quantity (21.48 mg) of that powder was dissolved in deionized water and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove any undissolved particles. The filtrate was then placed in a 1 L volumetric flask and deionized water was added to the mark, the resulting solution was stored for further analytical steps.

The analytical nanoprobe of MOX–CDs–Cu2+ was fabricated by mixing an aqueous solution of the prepared MOX–CDs with an equivalent volume of copper (II) chloride solution having a final concentration of 100 µM and incubating it for 10 min while adjusting the pH to 3.6 to 5.6 using acetate buffer which resulted in quenching of the fluorescence intensity by 73 %. The MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe solution was then mixed with various concentrations of standard or real sample PA solutions which resulted in recovery of the fluorescence intensity to different extents after an incubation period of 10 min. The entire procedure was conducted at ambient temperatures while vortex mixing was utilized to ensure homogenization of the different mixtures. The excitation wavelength of the fluorescence spectrum was at 410 nm while the emission spectrum was recorded between 420 to 650 nm.

The assay results of PA using the proposed method should be compared to pharmacopeial standard assay methods like the British Pharmacopeia (BP) to validate its accuracy and precision [39]. This standard assay method involves weighing and grinding the content of 20 PA capsules then weighing a quantity equal to 0.1 g of the active pharmaceutical ingredient and dissolving it in 50 mL of deionized water which is followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The pH of the filtrate is then alkalized by adding 5 mL of 1.0 M sodium hydroxide solution, then 0.2 mL of 0.1 % Dithizone solution is added which is followed by titration against 0.02 M Mercury (II) nitrate.

The stability studies of the MOX–CDs were conducted by measuring their fluorescence intensity in different conditions in terms of temperature, pH, and ionic strength. Evaluation of the thermal stability was achieved by heating samples of the MOX–CDs in an oil bath over the range of 25 °C to 150 °C for 15 min. A series of MOX–CDs aqueous solutions with different pH were prepared to evaluate the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, solutions of NaCl with various concentrations ranging from 0.1 M to 2.0 M were used to study the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated from the equation below while using fluorescein as the standard (QY = 95 %):
ϕ X = ϕ S
F x
.
A s
.
η x 2
F s
.
A x
.
η s 2
where ɸ refers to quantum yield, F refers to the area under the curve of the plotted fluorescence spectrum, while A and ɳ refer to the absorbance and solvent refractive indices respectively. The subscript X stands for sample while the subscript S stands for standard.

The size and morphology the prepared MOX–CDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 2 A, B), the MOX–CDs are generally monodispersed with an average diameter of around 5 nm and a size distribution range of 4 to 8 nm. Similarly, the AFM images (Fig. 2C) show that the MOX–CDs particles have a topographic height range of around 4 to 5.7 nm indicating that the particles are generally spherically shaped. Furthermore, the crystallinity of the MOX–CDs was studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD) which revealed a pattern with a broad peak at 2θ = 23.470 which is in strong relation with the poor crystalline nature of carbon dots (Fig. 2D) [40].

The functional groups and surface composition of the MOX–CDs were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in which the survey spectrum (Fig. 3 A) revealed four main element peaks: O 1 s at 533 eV, N 1 s at 401 eV, C 1 s at 286 eV, and P 2p at 135 eV with their content percentage being 50.52 %, 2.91 %, 38.89 %, and 7.68 % respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 3B) showed four peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, and 286.7 eV which correspond to C = C, C–C, C–O/C–N, and C = O respectively [41,42]. The deconvoluted O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3C) revealed a C = O peak at 532.2 eV, a P–O peak at 530.7 eV, and a C–O peak at 533.1 eV [43,44]. Furthermore, the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 3D) showed 2 peaks of C = N at 401.7 eV and C–NH at 400.2 eV while the P 2p spectra (Fig. 3E) demonstrated 2 peaks at 135.1 eV and 136.1 eV corresponding to P–O 2p1/2 and P–O 2p3/2 respectively which confirms the passivation of the MOX–CDs with phosphorus [45,46].

Comparing the infrared spectra (Fig. 4 A) of the MOX–CDs and the expired moxifloxacin precursor (EXP–MOX) could reveal the mechanisms by which the CDs were formulated as well disclosing any retained or added functional groups during the synthesis process. The MOX–CDs showed a very broad peak at 3700–2850 cm−1 which can be attributed to carboxylic acid O–H stretching vibrations while the EXP–MOX demonstrated a similar but less broad O–H peak at 3670–3070 cm−1 with two shoulders of N–H stretching that can be observed at 3510 at 3450 cm−1 [47,48]. The carbonyl peak of the MOX–CDs was observed at 1715 cm−1 while for the EXP–MOX it was recorded at a slightly lower wavenumber of 1700 cm−1 [49]. Both MOX–CDs and EXP–MOX showed C = C aromatic stretching peaks in the range of 1500 to 1650 cm−1 confirming that an aromatic ring or parts of it were retained on the MOX–CDs during the synthesis process [50]. In contrast, the EXP–MOX lacked the P–O stretching peak observed in MOX–CDs spectra at 1156 cm−1 which indicates that the CDs have undergone a surface passivation mechanism with phosphorus during their formation [51]. The formation of CDs is further supported by the Raman spectrum of the MOX–CDs (Fig. 4B) which showed the coexistence of the characteristic D band (sp3-hybridization) at 1385 cm−1 and G band (sp2-hybridization) at 1580 cm−1 with the ratio of ID/IG being 1.38 that can be attributed to defects resulting from the disordered amorphous carbon dots [52].

Comparing the optical properties of MOX–CDs and EXP–MOX is imperative as fluoroquinolone antibiotics like moxifloxacin are inherently fluorescent and the possibility of precursor-remnants-related fluorescence should be excluded [53]. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 4C) revealed that the MOX–CDs have an absorption shoulder at 251 nm which can be attributed to π – π* transition arising from the presence of C = C bonds [54]. Moreover, another peak can be observed at 326 nm which can be attributed to n–π* arising from the presence of C = O bonds [55]. Conversely, the EXP–MOX absorption spectra showed higher absorption intensity and shifting towards longer wavelength while having π – π* peak of the C = C group at 290 nm and n–π* peak of the C = O group at 360 nm. These differences in the absorption spectra can be due to the carbonization of MOX–CDs which decreases the structural conjugation by double bonds increasing the energy gap between orbitals requiring higher energy and lower wavelength for electron promotion [56].

Regarding fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 4D), the MOX–CDs demonstrated a strong emission peak at 510 nm upon excitation at 410 nm while showing excitation-independent fluorescence (Fig. 4E). The aqueous solution of the MOX–CDs appeared brown while observed in daylight and yellowish green upon exposure to UV light. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of EXP–MOX demonstrated a maximum emission peak at 487 nm with an excitation peak at 295 nm which are shifted towards a shorter wavelength when compared to MOX–CDs. The quantum yield of MOX–CDs and EXP–MOX was determined using a standard comparative method and was found to be around 51 % and 3 % respectively [57].

The wavelength differences between MOX–CDs and EXP–MOX in terms of absorption, excitation, and emission peaks as well as the quantum yield could originate from the potential mechanisms of formation of the MOX–CDs which could involve surface passivation and carbonization shifting the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra [58]. Consequently, the comparison between the functional groups and optical properties of MOX–CDs and EXP–MOX showed major differences confirming the formation of CDs while providing some insights on the potential synthesis mechanisms.

The fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs was recorded in different environments by changing pH, ionic strength, and temperature to evaluate their stability. The fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs did not show significant changes in the pH range of 3 to 9; however, it decreased notably when the pH was adjusted to extremely acidic or basic levels (Fig. 5 A) which can be due to protonation and deprotonation processes on the surface functional groups of the MOX–CDs [59]. Regarding the ionic strength effect, the MOX–CDs fluorescence intensity almost remained constant when placed in different NaCl solutions of concentrations ranging from 0.1–2.0 M (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs did not change when heated for 10 min in the range of 20 to 110 °C; however, it started to decrease beyond that range (Fig. 5C). The MOX–CDs showed high stability over a wide range of pH, ionic strength, and temperature which adds to its value as a robust probe in the pharmaceutical analysis field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Cu2+ on the MOX–CDs and fabrication of MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary studies showed that the MOX–CDs had only one significant interaction with metal ions which is quenching by copper (II) ions (Fig. 6 A), while it did not have any significant interactions with pharmaceutical excipients and PA (Fig. 6B). The interaction between the copper ions and the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the MOX–CDs leads to changes in electronic structure and charge transfer resulting in fluorescence quenching [60]. To determine the concentration of copper (II) ions required to achieve maximum quenching of the MOX–CDs, a series of solutions were prepared with different concentrations ranging from 1 to 150 µM. The fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs was quenched by 73 % of its initial value when the concentration of the added copper ions was around 100 µM (Fig. 6C) while ahigher concentration of copper (II) ions did not result in further quenching. These results led to the fabrication of the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe with the optimum concentration of copper (II) ions that would be utilized to detect and assay PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Penicillamine with the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of PA to the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ solution resulted in recovery of fluorescence intensity of up to 91 % via a turn ON-OFF-ON strategy with the maximum recovery being achieved after an incubation period of 10 min. This can be attributed to the powerful chelating actions of the thiol group of PA on the copper ions forming a complex (PA–Cu2+) which mitigates the quenching effects on the MOX–CDs photoluminescence [61]. Various concentrations of PA over a wide range were added to the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ solution to understand the characteristics of the probe (Fig. 6D). Determination of the linear range and the limit of detection was achieved by plotting F/F0 versus the concentration of PA where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm in the presence and absence of PA respectively (Fig. 6E). The lowest limit of detection (LOD) of PA was calculated using the 3σ/K equation where σ and K are the standard deviation and the slope of the calibration curve respectively in which the LOD was found to be 0.14 µM. The optimum linear range was determined by selecting the PA concentration points providing proportionality with F/F0 and having the highest coefficient of determination (R2) which was found to be from 2.5 to 50 µM with R2 = 0.9987. Analytical features of various approaches from the literature are listed in Table 1 and are compared to the proposed probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe for Penicillamine
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe for PA, the matrix effect of various pharmaceutical excipients should be assessed. This was achieved by adding high-concentration aliquots of these excipients to the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe and recording the fluorescence intensity. The pharmaceutical excipients did not demonstrate any significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of the MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe in comparison to PA which adds to the creditability of the proposed probe (Fig. 7 ).

The efficiency of the proposed probe can be assessed by using it for real sample analysis, for this purpose 250 mg Cupripen ® (Penicillamine) capsules (Laboratorios Rubió, Barcelona, Spain) would make a suitable candidate. The MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe was used to assay the content of PA capsules following the analytical producers detailed in section 2.5 using the standard addition method to minimize any errors. The assay was conducted in triplicate while comparing the results to the standard assay method detailed in section 2.6 in which each mL of the titrated mercury (II) nitrate was equivalent to 5.968 mg of PA. The analysis showed no significant differences (p > 0.05) between the proposed and standard assay methods as shown in Table 2 . The content assay of PA capsules ranged from 249.14 mg to 256.12 mg (which stands for 99.6 % to 102.44 % of the label-stated content) with an average weight of 251.65 mg which lies within the acceptable content range of 95 % to 105 % indicated by the British Pharmacopeia. Based on the results, the proposed method can be used as an alternative to the standard methods of assay of PA in pharmaceutical samples as it provides high sensitivity and accuracy while being relatively cheap and simple.

In conclusion, the utilization of expired drug powder as a novel carbon-source precursor was successful, and carbon dots (MOX–CDs) were synthesized via a facile one-step solvothermal method. The synthesized MOX–CDs exhibited excellent luminescence properties with a quantum yield of around 51 % and excitation-independent fluorescence while being highly stable in extreme pH, temperature, and ionic strength. Moreover, the MOX–CDs were used to fabricate a probe (MOX–CDs–Cu2+) that can be applied for the quantification of PA with a unique fluorescence turn ON-OFF-ON strategy. The fabricated MOX–CDs–Cu2+ probe demonstrated interesting characteristics in terms of PA assay including a low detection limit of 0.14 µM and a linear range of 2.5–50 µM while it did not show any significant interactions with pharmaceutical excipients. Finally, the proposed approach not only offered simple, accurate, and sensitive means of determining PA, but it also provided insights about recycling expired drug waste into carbon dots contributing to environmental sustainability.
This work did not receive any grants from external funding agencies.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to acknowledge the invaluable support of the head and staff of the Center of Biomedical Analysis, Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani as well as the head and staff of the research center of Komar University.

Talanta.
